Playbook.
Changing youth soccer in the Twin Cities — the positioning, the personas, the launch film, the content engine, the capture system, and the first ninety days. Built to entertain, inform, and inspire.

Anecdotal × Salvo SC
Landscape Positioning Personas Kick-Off System Ideas Capture Calendar Rollout
01
Where We Are
The Landscape.
US youth soccer is in the middle of its loudest-ever cost debate — in a World Cup year — while every Twin Cities club posts the same announcement graphics. That combination is the opportunity.

The state of play: pay-to-play is the story.

$0
Average annual family spend on youth soccer — more than basketball or baseball (Aspen Institute, 2024)
$8–0K
Typical all-in yearly cost at elite ECNL / GA / MLS NEXT level
0K
Kids lost by US youth soccer over five years as costs rose ~46% (2019–24)
0×
How much more likely kids from $100K+ households are to play travel sports vs. under-$50K households

The cultural moment: CNN ran "How the cost of youth soccer blew out of control" this month — $3,500 club fees for 9-year-olds made national news. Reddit parent threads, the I Hate Soccer podcast, and World Cup discourse are all circling the same question. Some think the model just needs tweaks; more and more want real change. Almost no club is answering in public. Salvo can.

What every Twin Cities club posts.

St. Croix SC — the acronym graphic. Pre-ECNL, ECNL-RL, grunge type. Meaningless to a new family.
Tonka United — the game-day template. Pro-style polish, interchangeable with any club in America.
Eden Prairie SC — the trophy post. Every win, every time, same layout.
Receipts — all three pulled from competitor feeds this month (July 2026)
The template everyone runs
  • Announcement graphics, tryout reminders, trophy photos.
  • Trading-card / "every player is a superstar" edits — the identical flex, club after club.
  • Bios that lead with acronyms: ECNL-RL · NPL · TCSL · GA — meaningless to a new family, and confusing if you're not already in the sport.
  • A pompous "elite / premier" tone, with little diversity visible in the region's feeds.
What nobody is doing
  • Taking a stance on cost — transparency, financial aid, the pay-to-play debate.
  • Kid-facing culture content: FUT cards, EA FC crossovers, rankings debates.
  • Explainers parents demonstrably search for: the MN league pyramid, tryouts, the high-school rule.
  • A women's-pathway story. Salvo's is the best in the market — and it's underexposed.

Competitor snapshot.

ClubIG FollowingWhat Their Feed IsRead
MNUFC Academy
~17,000Pro-grade announcement graphics, signingsSets the local production bar. That polish = "legit" in parents' and kids' minds.
MN Thunder Academy
~5,000Acronym-led elite-pathway positioningCredentials-first, personality-free.
Minneapolis United
~3,600Club info; new MLS NEXT Academy DivisionTheir MLS NEXT news will earn attention this fall.
St. Croix SC
~3,500Most active locally; coach-intro reels, tryouts; separate USL2 accountThe busiest account — but still announcement-driven.
Salvo SC
~3,300 (+ ~760 WPSL side account)Announcements, league credentialsUs, today. Solid base, undifferentiated voice.
Tonka United
~2,500Standard club info — but on TikTokBiggest club in the west metro; the TikTok lane is contested but not dominated.
Eden Prairie SC
ModestProgram-catalog contentFunctional, not emotional.

Unclaimed ground.

Let's be precise about what these are. None of them make Salvo automatically better — other clubs have scholarships too, and the MSHSL rule applies to everyone. What they share is this: nobody in the market is telling these stories, and Salvo is the most credible teller of each. That's the claim — and it holds up in a boardroom.
The exclusive asset
The WPSL story

2023 WPSL national runner-up. Conference champs in 2019 and 2022, a roster almost entirely Minnesota-grown, headquartered inside TCO Stadium. This is the one item no competitor can copy or catch — but an asset only becomes an advantage when it's activated, and today it sits on a 756-follower side account. Telling this story is the advantage.

The stance others won't copy
Access & transparency

Honesty check: most metro clubs have financial aid — Salvo isn't unique for having it, and the majority-girls roster is by default, not by design. The differentiation is the willingness to lead with it publicly: transparent pricing, aid said out loud, diversity named. Competitors could copy this tomorrow. Their "elite / premier" positioning means they won't — and being first, loudest, and most consistent is what makes it ours.

The lane nobody claims
The Minnesota quirk

MSHSL Bylaw 208.1 bars playing club and high school soccer at the same time in-season — and it applies to every Minnesota club equally, so there's no edge in the rule itself. The edge is first-mover: nobody explains it well, parents are confused by it, and the club that owns the explainer lane earns the authority halo. Note: it's an MSHSL bylaw, not a state law — the explainer must get this right.

Immediate housekeeping wins: claim a Salvo TikTok (we're absent; Tonka isn't) · fold the WPSL account into one cross-promoted brand system · rewrite the IG bio to lead with the mission, not the acronyms.

02
Who We Are Online
Positioning & Voice.
Every competitor posts the same three things: announcement graphics, trading-card edits, and trophy photos. Salvo's feed should feel like a club with a point of view. Four positioning pillars carry every piece of content.
1
A stance on pay-to-play

The cost conversation is the loudest conversation in youth soccer right now, and almost no club is willing to touch it. Salvo takes a clear, constructive stance: soccer should be accessible, and here's what we're doing about it — scholarships, financial assistance, transparent pricing. This is the strongest differentiator available to us, and it's true.

2
Diversity, named out loud

The club's goal is a more diverse membership. We don't skirt it — we name it, and then we prove it visually: who's in the photos, whose families are interviewed, which neighborhoods get shouted out, what languages appear in captions. And we don't shy away from international soccer — it's the front door to the families we most want to reach.

3
Show, don't say

Verbally, we position around changing youth soccer. Visually, we let the winning, the development, and the joy speak. We never caption-brag about being competitive — we show a bi-weekly highlight reel that makes it obvious. Actions on screen, mission in the voiceover.

4
Unmistakably Twin Cities

Neighborhood callouts, local idols, lake-and-winter humor, Rosemount / Two Rivers / Woodbury pride, Minnesota-specific explainers. Local flavor is woven in periodically, not every post — enough that a Minneapolis parent feels this could only be their club.

The humility clause: "We don't have all the answers — we're trying to figure it out, with the best people we could find who care about the same mission." This line of thinking keeps the mission talk from sounding pompous. Confidence without arrogance is the whole voice.

Tone of voice.

Confident

We know ball. Coaching content is delivered with authority — our coaches talk to other coaches as peers.

Playful

Humor in moments, not as a costume. Meme fluency, gaming references, kids roasting coaches' rankings.

Proud & warm

Proud of players, staff, and place. Warm to newcomers — zero insider gatekeeping, no jargon without an explainer.

Honest

We highlight honest moments, not just wins — tough calls, hard losses, nervous first tryouts. Honesty is what earns the brag.

Sounds like us
  • "If you care about youth soccer in the Twin Cities, follow along — here's what we're trying to do."
  • "We asked the coaches for the best position on the field. Then we asked the U12s. It got interesting."
  • "Real question from a parent this week — here's the honest answer."
  • "New kits. That's it. That's the post."
Never sounds like us
  • "Elite. Premier. Dominant." — pompous positioning language with nothing behind it.
  • Every player framed as a superstar — the trading-card cliché every club runs.
  • Chest-thumping after every single win. Wins are shown, on a rhythm, not shouted.
  • Corporate-speak about "development pathways" with no explanation for new families.

Look & feel — with examples.

A warmer take on the club's colors: darker blues grounded with oranges and yellows, gradient and texture over flat corporate white — Nashville SC's gold-on-navy energy, made Minnesotan. Below: the palette, and two example treatments built with real Salvo photos.
Deep Navy#12233F · the ground
Twilight Blue#1D3557 · surfaces
Salvo Orange#E0592A · heat
Warm Gold#F5A83C · the accent
Warm Cream#FFF4E0 · light & type

The type.

Headline · Anton
Match day
in the cities.
The hype voice — announcements, kit drops, milestone posts. All caps, tight, loud.
Headline · Barlow Semi Condensed
Fall registration is open — all three branches
The workhorse headline — explainers, carousels, everyday posts. 600/700 weight.
Body · DM Sans
Financial assistance is real, tryouts are free, and every player gets seen. Ask us anything in the DMs.
Captions, body copy, detail lines. Friendly, legible at feed size.
Example · Tweet-style announcement — navy over photo, DM Sans body
Example · New-hire graphic — Anton headline, orange-to-navy gradient + texture
Graphics

ESPN-style announcement layouts, motion blur, textured static posts. Big condensed type, one face, one hook — never three messages on one card.

Photography

Groups over solo hero shots. Serious and fun in the same set. The diversity of the club must be visible in every grid row without ever feeling staged.

Edits

Transition-edit language for hype moments: jersey-swap on a clap, whip-pan match cuts, slow-motion ramps on the strike, ball-rolls-over-the-lens reveals.

03
Who We're Talking To
Five Personas.
Personas aren't demographic labels — they're decision frameworks. Before any post ships, it should have an obvious primary persona. Open a card for the full strategy.
Two Rivers session · the club the feed should look like
Persona 01 · Priority
Amina & Family — New American Parents
"Soccer is our sport. But these clubs — the prices, the process — it doesn't feel like it's for us."
Somali · Hispanic · East African communitiesWhatsApp + Facebook + IG
Who they are

Families where soccer is cultural identity, not an extracurricular. Deeply cost-conscious and trust-driven — they move on word of mouth from their own community, not ads. They often assume club soccer is financially and culturally closed to them.

What they need to see

Faces and names like theirs already at Salvo. Plain-language costs and financial assistance, said out loud without shame. And their soccer world on our feed — international football treated as a first language, not a novelty.

Full strategy
Platforms

Facebook and WhatsApp shares first; IG for the kids. Content must survive being forwarded — self-explanatory, captioned, warm.

Content that works

Affordability explainers, parent testimonials, Match Day Around the World, My First Jersey, multilingual captions on key registration posts.

CTAs that work

"Financial assistance is real — here's the link." / "Ask us anything, in any language, in the DMs."

Tone

Respectful, direct, zero fine print. Never charity-toned — this is their sport; we're the ones catching up.

Persona 02
Maya — The Young Female Player
"Every soccer edit on my feed is boys. Where's ours?"
Age 11–17TikTok + IG nativeSalvo's actual majority
Who she is

Salvo has more female players than male — by default, not by design. Maya lives in soccer-video subculture: edits, transitions, skill challenges, EA FC. She's underserved by a content landscape that centers boys.

What she needs to see

Girls' teams getting the same premium-edit treatment as anyone. Her name, team, and position on screen. The WPSL team as a visible ceiling: proof the pathway at this club goes somewhere.

Full strategy
Platforms

TikTok and IG Reels; carousels of game-day photo sets she'll tag friends in.

Content that works

Skill challenges, transition edits, mic'd-up moments, rankings debates, WPSL player crossovers with youth teams.

CTAs that work

"Tag someone who could hit this." / "Duet your attempt."

Tone

Peer-level, fast, funny. Never adult-explaining-the-internet.

Persona 03
Dave & Jen — The Evaluating Parents
"Everyone says their club is elite. What does it actually cost, and will my kid actually develop?"
Comparing 3–4 clubsFacebook + Google + Reddit
Who they are

The parents in the Twin Cities youth soccer Reddit threads asking about cost, tryouts, travel load, and club-vs-rec. Skeptical of superstar marketing. Confused by the academy / league / level alphabet soup — ECNL, NPL, GA, USYS mean nothing to them.

What they need to see

Clear communication: what the academies and levels actually are, how tryouts work, what a season costs, how the Minnesota high-school rule affects the calendar. Coaches who visibly know their stuff.

Full strategy
Platforms

Facebook and YouTube for explainers; they'll binge an informative series before a tryout decision.

Content that works

"How club soccer actually works in MN" series, coach drill breakdowns, honest Q&A, tryout-week guides.

CTAs that work

"Full breakdown at the link." / "Drop your question — we answer all of them."

Tone

Informative, confident, light-hearted — the no-brainer voice. Answer the question they were afraid to ask.

Persona 04
Coach T & The Insiders — Staff, Volunteers, Club Families
"I'd share every post if they gave me something worth sharing."
Already followingUnderactivated amplifiers
Who they are

Current families, coaches, volunteers, and the team-level social media managers. Our distribution network — every share lands in exactly the local networks we want. Currently under-armed.

What they need to see

Their kid, their team, their coach — by name. Content built to be reshared to a story in one tap. Recognition of staff expertise, not just player highlights.

Full strategy
Platforms

IG stories and Facebook — where proud parents already live.

Content that works

Bi-weekly highlight reels, name/team/position graphics, coach spotlight clips, throwback photos of coaches' playing days.

CTAs that work

"Share to your story if this is your squad." / "Send us your clips."

Tone

Celebratory, generous with credit, inside-jokes welcome.

Persona 05
Ms. Ohlson — Donors & Community Stakeholders
"Show me the impact, not a grant proposal."
Sponsors · foundations · city partnersLinkedIn + FB + local media
Who they are

Mission-driven funders and partners who make the scholarship fund — and therefore the accessibility stance — real. A grant PDF can't show them a kid's first season; the feed can.

What they need to see

Impact storytelling: scholarship stories (told with dignity and permission), participation numbers, the mini-documentaries. Proof this club is the credible vehicle for growing the game in underserved Twin Cities communities.

Full strategy
Platforms

Facebook and YouTube; mini-docs double as pitch assets in sponsorship meetings.

Content that works

Mini-documentaries, season-impact recaps, "what your support built" posts, earned local-media moments.

CTAs that work

"Partner with us — help us grow the game." / "Support the scholarship fund."

Tone

Sincere, evidence-led, never saccharine.

04
The First Posts
Kick-Off Content.
The first video is a brand move — a statement of what we believe, not an announcement and not a callout. Confident, warm, collaborative. It sets the terms for everything that follows and gives every future post a "that's what we're trying to do here" to point back to.
Saturday morning — where every Salvo story starts
The concept

A 60–90 second mission film. A single warm voiceover (Alex is the leading candidate) over b-roll of real club life: kids arriving, parents tying cleats, coaches greeting players by name, the WPSL women warming up. The voice carries the belief; the footage quietly shows competitive quality and joy. We never say "we win" — we show it.

It opens with the players, not a critique — and closes with an invitation, not a mic drop.

The launch package · pinned everywhere

Two assets go live together and get pinned to the top of every channel:

  • The mission film — the belief, in 90 seconds.
  • The club carousel — one card per side of Salvo: rec, competitive, the Academy, camps & training, the leagues, the tournaments, the WPSL team, financial aid. A new family understands the whole club in one swipe.

The script — mission film, 60–90 seconds.

Open

"Youth soccer has become a conversation about a lot of things.

We think it should start with the players."

Kids arriving. Parents tying cleats. Coaches greeting players by name.
Belief

"Because every child deserves the chance to discover what this game can teach…

Confidence. Resilience. Teamwork.

Friendships that last far beyond the final whistle."

Training, laughter, encouragement, small victories.
Our approach

"So every decision we make starts with one question…

'What's best for the player?'

That means creating more opportunities for families to be part of the game, surrounding players with exceptional coaches, and building an environment where every athlete can grow."

"More opportunities" subtly nods to accessibility without criticizing anyone.
Community

"We're proud to share the Twin Cities with clubs that love this game as much as we do.

Because when soccer grows here, every player benefits."

Handshakes after matches, opposing teams, WPSL, community events.
Ending

"The future of youth soccer won't be built by one club.

It'll be built by every coach who teaches the game the right way… every family who believes in their child… and every player willing to put in the work.

At Salvo, that's the future we're working toward every day.

If you believe in that too, we'd love to have you follow along."

Wide of a full field at golden hour; the badge; handle lockup.
Shot list priorities

Diversity visible without being staged. Girls' teams prominent. Winter/indoor Minnesota texture. One genuinely great goal, one genuinely great save, one genuine celebration pile-up. Faces over drone shots.

Distribution

Native upload everywhere on the same morning: IG Reel, TikTok, Facebook Reel, YouTube, X — film and club carousel pinned on every channel. Staff and parents briefed to share day one.

Guardrails

No sad-piano framing, no "youth soccer is broken" opener, no criticizing anyone. Other clubs appear only as partners in growing the game. The claim is direction, not arrival.

05
The Engine
The Content System.
Every post exists for a reason: entertain, inform, or inspire — ideally two at once. Content types are assigned per objective so the feed has rhythm instead of noise.

Objectives → formats → cadence.

ObjectiveFormatCadence & RulePrimary Persona
Brand beliefMission films, "what we're trying to do" carouselsLaunch, then quarterly. Every other post points back to it.All
Wins & resultsBi-weekly highlight reel with a premium editWins are not celebrated individually. They roll up into the reel — unless it's a major milestone (championship, first-ever, WPSL moment), which gets its own ESPN-style post.Insiders, Maya
AnnouncementsTiered by priority — see the three levels belowAs needed. The tier is decided before design starts, not after.Evaluating parents, Insiders
Inform & explainYouTube/Reel explainer series, FAQ carouselsWeekly slot. Academies, leagues, levels, tryouts, costs, the MN high-school rule, gear and rules knowledge.Dave & Jen, Amina
EntertainRankings, challenges, memes, EA FC cultureTwice weekly. This is the reach engine — engineered for shares and the FYP.Maya, players
InspireMini-docs, storytelling, pep talks, mic'd upBi-weekly. Pre-scouted subjects with real backstories.Amina, donors
Staff authorityCoach tips, drill breakdowns, messages to other coachesWeekly. Our coaches talk like the experts they are.Dave & Jen, coaching community

Announcements run on three levels.

Priority is assigned when the news exists, not when the design starts. The level decides the format, the platforms, and the effort.
1
The full send

A sport-edit hype video plus carousel plus stories, on every platform the same day. Reserved for news that deserves an audience even if you don't care about the club.

Kit drops · WPSL milestones · championships · major hires
2
The ESPN treatment

Polished static or carousel in the announcement design system — big type, warm navy/gold, one message per card. Feed post plus story reshare.

Registration windows · new programs · staff additions · tournament news
3
Story only

Time-sensitive or internal-facing information lives in stories and dies in 24 hours. It never clogs the grid.

Schedule changes · weather calls · reminders · countdowns

The carousel is the new thumbnail.

Card 1 · Photo — the hook
Card 2 · Video — the payoff
Card 3 · Photo — the moment
Card 4 · Video — the energy
Come play
with us.
Register → link in bio
Card 5 · The call-to-action
The anatomy of a Salvo carousel

Instagram is pushing carousels the way YouTube pushes thumbnails — card 1 does the job a thumbnail does: bold type, one face, one hook, earn the swipe. Then the rhythm is fixed: photo → video → photo → video → call-to-action. On a previous Anecdotal client this format outperformed single photos roughly 2:1 (31K vs ~15K views).

1 / 5

The craft rules — how videos get made.

The validating thought

Structure videos so the viewer arrives at the thought just before we say it — a slow build to "the best position on the field is…" that they're already yelling at the screen. Validation is retention. This is a build technique for many videos, not a single format.

The coach VO relay

Coaches tell us exactly what they're doing — "we're setting up set pieces for Saturday" — and if the on-field delivery isn't clean, Jarod re-records the same explanation as the voiceover. Expertise from the source, polish from the studio.

The at-home pipeline

Kids are prompted to film at home — driveway touches, wall passes, celebrations — and tag the club. It's a standing UGC supply line and reach into friend groups we can't buy, harvested for challenges and montages.

The knowledge principle

Do our followers already know most of what an explainer covers? Yes. They watch anyway — because they love the sport, want to go deeper, or want to test themselves. Personal investment is what primes the algorithm.

Platform playbook.

Role · the flagship grid

IG is pushing carousels hard and deprioritizing Reels — so carousels are our lead format, built to the anatomy above. Stories carry Level-3 announcements, reshares, and polls.

Rules
  • Carousel-first for photo sets, rankings recaps, explainers.
  • Reels still run — challenges, edits, mic'd up — but carousels get the premium design time.
  • Every human on screen gets name · team · position.
  • Pin: kick-off film, "start here" explainer carousel, current registration post.
Role · reach & youth culture

TikTok is TikTok — the FYP rewards native energy, not polish. This is where rankings debates, skill challenges, EA FC crossovers, memes, and transition edits live. Players' faces and voices, not the club logo, carry it.

Rules
  • Hook in the first 1.5 seconds — motion or a bold claim.
  • Trend adaptation weekly: we soccer-ify the sound or format within 48 hours, or skip it.
  • Reply to comments with video — a coach settling a rankings argument is a free second post.
  • Nothing that requires knowing the club to enjoy.
Role · parents & community

Facebook loves Reels and is where evaluating parents and community elders actually are. Highlight reels, explainers, registration info, and shareable pride posts. Note: IG carousels don't cross-post cleanly to FB — FB versions get rebuilt as Reels or single-image + link.

Rules
  • Reels-first; static posts only for time-sensitive info.
  • Captions carry more text here — parents read.
  • Registration and financial-assistance posts always include a plain link and a "DM us in any language" line.
  • Feed the neighborhood groups: content built to be shared into them, never spammed by us.
Role · the library

YouTube loves informative series. This is the permanent home for the explainer series ("How club soccer actually works in Minnesota"), coach drill breakdowns, mini-documentaries, and full highlight reels. It's what a parent binges the week before tryouts.

Rules
  • Series over one-offs: consistent titles, numbered episodes, one thumbnail system.
  • Shorts = repurposed TikToks; longform = docs and explainers (3–8 min).
  • Every video description links registration + financial assistance.
Role · scoreboard & soccer talk

A low-lift presence: ESPN-style announcement graphics, live-ish score updates during tournaments, WPSL results, and joining Minnesota soccer conversation with personality.

Rules
  • Announcement graphics reposted from the design system — no bespoke work.
  • One voice: dry, quick, local. Banter yes, beefing no.
06
The Shelf
Content Idea Library.
Forty ready-to-run ideas, filterable by series. Each one names its format and its hook logic. The calendar in section 08 pulls directly from this shelf.
RankingsTikTok · Reels
Coaches vs. Kids: Best Position on the Field

Ask coaches, then players — names and positions on screen. Generational disagreement is the engine; the comments finish the video for us.

→ "Settle it in the comments."
RankingsTikTok · Reels
Best Player of All Time — U10 Edition

Kids passionately defending Messi / Ronaldo / Mbappé / "my cousin." Inherently funny, endlessly rewatchable, zero production cost.

→ "Who told him that??"
RankingsReels · Carousel
Best Players in History — World Edition

The same question, asked across the whole club — and answered from every soccer culture in it: Okocha, Valderrama, Hugo Sánchez, Eto'o, Marta, Mia Hamm. The point is the diversity of the answers; the debate is the reach.

→ "Your dad's GOAT vs. your GOAT."
RankingsReels · TikTok
Best Way to Finish — World Edition

Panenka, trivela, chip, laces through it, olimpico — players and coaches from different footballing traditions demo and defend their finish. Diverse perspectives, then the comments vote.

→ "Defend your finish."
RankingsCarousel
Rank the Finish

Coaches rank five real goals from the week's sessions, S-tier to C-tier. Bi-weekly. Players lobby for regrades in the comments — that's the point.

→ "He got robbed at #3."
RankingsTikTok
Favorite Formation Debate

Coaches explain their favorite formation in 20 seconds; a 12-year-old explains why they're wrong. Authority plus comedy in one clip.

→ "4-3-3 propaganda continues."
ChallengeTikTok · Reels
First to Hit the Shot

Crossbar / top-bins / trick-shot ladder. First kid to make it wins bragging rights and a feature. Assigned to the strongest team's social manager.

→ "Duet your attempt."
ChallengeTikTok
Touch Challenge, Coach-Rated

Players do a touch or juggling sequence; a coach rates it out of 10 with one line of real feedback. Skill content plus coach authority in one.

→ "A 7?? That was a 9."
ChallengeReels
Cone Drill That Ends in a Finish

The capture rule in action: drills chosen because they end in something inherently watchable — a strike, a save, a celebration.

→ "Try this at practice."
Mini-docYouTube · cutdowns
The Coach Who Played Pro

A pre-scouted subject with a real backstory. 4–6 minute doc, cut into three vertical teasers. Subjects identified in advance, not found in the edit.

→ "Full story on YouTube."
Mic'd upReels · TikTok
Mic'd-Up Coach

NFL-style: coach mic'd at practice — encouragement, jokes, teaching moments. Kids are naturals; adults warm up by episode two.

→ "Wait for the pep talk."
StorytellingCarousel · Reel
First Season Stories

A scholarship family's first year, told with dignity and permission. The accessibility stance made human — and the strongest donor asset we can make.

→ "This is why the fund exists."
StorytellingReels
Wisdom From a Kid

The things we want to say, said by a 10-year-old: why they love their team, what a good teammate is. Lands harder from them than from us.

→ "Out of the mouths of babes."
ThrowbackCarousel
Coaches' Playing Days

Old match photos of coaches and staff — a proven performer from past clients. Pairs with a one-line "what I'd tell 15-year-old me."

→ "Guess the coach."
Honest momentsReel
After the Loss

The honest moments, not just the wins: how coaches help players handle the emotions after a hard defeat — the quiet bus, the next-day reset, the "this is development too" talk. The content every sports parent quietly needs.

→ "What we say after a loss."
ExplainerYouTube series
How Club Soccer Actually Works in MN

The flagship informative series: the academies, the leagues, the levels, the ECNL/NPL/GA alphabet soup, tryouts, costs. One question per episode, answered like a friend would.

→ "Ep. 1: What even is 'club' soccer?"
ExplainerReel · Carousel
The Minnesota High-School Rule

Minnesota's club-vs-high-school season rule is genuinely confusing to outsiders and unique nationally. A perfect explainer: local, surprising, useful.

→ "Yes, this is a Minnesota thing."
ExplainerReel
Why Was That a Foul?

Rules, tough calls, and controversy from real (anonymized) game moments. "Why does this happen?" content is inherently interesting — and parents quietly need it.

→ "The ref was right. Here's why."
KnowledgeReel · Carousel
Why Is the Goal That Size?

4v4 pop-ups, 9v9 goals, full-size frames — why each league and age group plays with the goal it does, and what that does to development. Sport nerdery that fans of the game can't not watch.

→ "Bet you never asked why."
KnowledgeReel
Size 3, 4, 5 — Balls, Explained

Why a U8 ball isn't a small adult ball, what a futsal ball teaches your feet, why the World Cup ball is controversial every four years. Test-your-knowledge framing built in.

→ "Which size does U11 play? No googling."
KnowledgeReel · Carousel
Cleats, Explained

FG vs AG vs turf vs indoor, how they should actually fit, and what you genuinely don't need to spend. Half explainer, half quiet affordability content.

→ "The $40 boot vs the $240 boot."
ExplainerCarousel · FB
What It Actually Costs

A transparent cost breakdown and financial-assistance walkthrough. Nobody in the market does this. Directly serves the pay-to-play stance, with receipts.

→ "Financial aid is real. Here's the link."
ExplainerReel
What Is the WPSL?

Explain the women's league Salvo competes in and what it means for a 12-year-old girl at the club: the pathway has a visible top floor.

→ "From U10 to the W."
GamingTikTok · Reels
Rate These Lineups: FC 26 Edition

Players build their EA FC 26 Ultimate Team lineups; teammates and coaches rate them on camera. The debate is the content — "no defenders?? zero?" — and it's native to how they already play.

→ "Drop your XI below."
GamingTikTok · Reels
Career Mode: Salvo Edition

Player intros shot like an EA FC career-mode signing: stats card, position, signature move, weak foot rating (self-reported, disputed by teammates). Every player gets name · team · position by design.

→ "His weak foot is NOT 4 stars."
MemeAll platforms
Meme Adaptations

Weekly trend watch: soccer-ify the format within 48 hours or skip. Sounds, templates, edits — executed by someone fluent, never explained.

→ (the meme is the caption)
EditReels · TikTok
Transition Edit of the Month

One premium edit per month. Examples of the language: jersey-swap on a clap, boot-tap covers the lens, whip-pan match cut from practice to game day, ball rolls over the lens into a new scene, slow-mo ramp on the strike, tunnel walkout to kickoff.

→ "Run it back in slow motion."
Coach tipReel · YouTube
One Drill, One Minute

A coach explains a drill and why it works — the voiceover can be Jarod re-recording the coach's own explanation for clarity. Authority content other coaches will share.

→ "Steal this drill."
Coach tipReel
Messages to Other Coaches

Our staff talking craft to the coaching community — set pieces, conditioning, managing parents. Positions Salvo staff as the best in their field, which is the hiring pitch too.

→ "Coaches, thoughts?"
AnalogyTikTok · Reel
Soccer, Explained by Analogy

"A 6 is the group-project kid who does everything." Analogy videos make tactics legible to non-soccer parents and funny to players.

→ "Which one are you?"
HypeReel + carousel
Kit Drop

New kits get the full streetwear treatment: teaser story, reveal edit with motion blur, player photoshoot carousel. A Level-1 announcement, always.

→ "Cop yours at registration."
HypeReel
Announcement as Sport Edit

Registration dates, new staff, tournament news — delivered inside a hype edit our audience will watch anyway. The information rides the entertainment.

→ "Dates in the caption."
HighlightsReel · FB
The Bi-Weekly Reel

All wins, golazos, and saves from two weeks, one edit, every team represented over a month's span. The disciplined alternative to celebrating every win individually.

→ "Were you there?"
CampsReel + carousel · FB
Camps & Training Drops

Summer camps and winter age-group training get their own launch rhythm: a Level-2 announcement when registration opens, then camp-week recap reels that sell next season better than any flyer. Salvo runs both — most families only know about one.

→ "Summer camps + winter training — link in bio."
TournamentsAll platforms
Tournament Central

Salvo hosts a tournament for every season — Fall, Spring, and Summer Classics, the Girls Diamond College Showcase, REV IT UP! Each gets the sport-edit hype announcement, then festival-style weekend coverage. Sign-up content and community content from the same event.

→ "One for every season. Get in."
WorldReel · FB
Match Day Around the World

Salvo families share who they support and how match day looks at home — Liga MX Sundays, Premier League mornings, AFCON runs, Gold Cup nights. International soccer is the front door for the families we most want to reach; we walk through it proudly.

→ "Who does your house support?"
WorldCarousel · Reel
My First Jersey

Players and parents tell the story of the first jersey in their family — where it came from, who wore it, what it meant. A diversity story told through fabric, not statements.

→ "Bring yours to practice."
WorldAll platforms
World Cup Corner

The 2026 World Cup is on this continent right now — kids react to results, pick their nations, recreate goals at practice. The biggest soccer conversation on earth, joined from a Twin Cities pitch.

→ "Recreating THAT goal, badly."
LocalCarousel
Neighborhood Callouts

"This week's golazo brought to you by Rosemount." Local idols, lake jokes, winter-training content. Periodic, not every post — seasoning, not the dish.

→ "Rep your side of the river."
LocalReel · FB
Game Day in the Community

Tournament weekends (Fall Classic, showcases) covered like festivals: food, families, languages, sidelines. The diversity pillar shown, never announced.

→ "Sound on for this one."

Why knowledge content works: most of our audience already knows what a size 4 ball is. They watch anyway — because they love the sport, want to go a level deeper, or want to test themselves. That personal investment produces the watch time and saves that prime the algorithm for everything else we post.

07
The Hands
The Capture Playbook.
Anecdotal directs and edits; team-level social media managers capture. This is the operating manual that turns amateurs into a reliable footage supply — clear instructions, simple criteria, and real-time backup when they need it.

How the system works.

1
One manager per team

Each team gets a dedicated social media manager reporting to Anecdotal. They own capture only — they are never responsible for inventing video concepts. Creative direction, scripting, storyboarding, and editing stay with Anecdotal.

2
Briefed, then backed up

Onboarding one-on-one over Zoom. Before anything interesting — a game, a themed practice, a tournament — a quick call the night before: here's the schedule, here's exactly what to shoot. During the shoot: WhatsApp for questions, FaceTime if framing needs a live check.

3
Matched to skill level

Confident shooters get the video idea and creative freedom. Novices get hands-on, shot-by-shot instructions. Everyone uploads to the shared library same-day, labeled with team and date.

Pick the shot type — the cameras move.

Every video type gets its own setup, and every brief names it — managers never have to guess. Tap a shot type below and the field shows exactly where the phones go.

Camera criteria — the six rules.

01 · Shoot 4K

4K, highest frame rate available for action (slow-motion insurance). Lens wiped, phone horizontal for YouTube masters, vertical for everything else — the brief will say which.

02 · Fill the frame

The #1 amateur mistake is standing too far back. If the player doesn't fill a third of the frame, walk closer. Faces and boots beat wide grass.

03 · Positions that work

Use the shot-type selector above — it maps every video type to its setup. When in doubt, corner of the 18.

04 · Vary the shot type

Chase shots (walk or jog with the action), stationary tripod shots, low angles, detail shots — boots, ball, hands, bench reactions. Every session should return at least three of the four.

05 · Shoot what's interesting

If a drill will produce something watchable — a cone drill that ends in a finish, a keeper battle, a nutmeg game — shoot it. If it won't, skip it. "Interesting in real life" is the filter.

06 · The caption rule

Any time a person is featured: capture their name, team, and position (ask on the spot, note it in the upload). On-screen identity is house style — it's also what makes families share.

Sound, interviews & mic'd up.

Audio setup
  • Ask upfront who's comfortable on camera — build the on-camera roster around them. The club purchases wireless mics for those individuals.
  • Kids are naturally better on camera than adult coaches. Lean into it — the things we want to say land harder coming from a kid.
  • Mic'd-up sessions: mic the coach at practice, NFL Films style. Candid coaching, jokes, pep talks. One per team per month.
  • Meta glasses for POV b-roll — dribbling runs, shooting drills, a keeper's-eye view.
Interview quick rules
  • Quiet-ish background, subject facing the light, mic on, phone at eye level, fill the frame.
  • Have subjects include the question in the answer ("My favorite position is…").
  • Let silence sit for two beats — the best line comes after the pause.
  • Kids get fast, fun questions (best player ever? best finish?). Adults get why questions (why this drill? why this club?).

The coach voiceover relay, on the ground: coaches just explain what they're doing and why — "we're setting up set pieces for Saturday," "this is conditioning, here's what it builds" — on camera or straight into a phone memo. If the delivery isn't usable, Jarod re-records the same explanation as a clean voiceover in the edit. Expertise from the source, polish from the studio.

The pre-shoot brief — five questions.

Every planned shoot gets this brief from Anecdotal before the night-before call. If a question has no answer, the shoot isn't ready.
1
The moment

What specific thing are we capturing? Not "practice" — "the 1v1 ladder that ends in finishing."

2
The story use

Which idea or series from the library does this feed, and which persona is it for?

3
The shots

A named shot list: positions, chase vs. stationary, details. Minimum five clips per shot.

4
The sound

Who's mic'd? What question or explanation do we need on tape?

5
The people

Who's featured (name · team · position), are they comfortable, and is their media waiver on file?

08
The Rhythm
90-Day Content Calendar.
Season one: August–October 2026. Three posts a week in month one, four a week from month two — quality over volume, every post pulled from the idea library. High-school season (mid-August through October) means older players are with their schools: content leans on younger teams, the WPSL story, coaches, and explainers.
Theme: plant the flag. Launch the kick-off film, say what we believe, start the explainer series, and set the entertainment engine running. Goal: establish the voice, TikTok live.
Week 1 — The Flag
Mission film premieres (pinned everywhere)
All platforms · 75 sec
The club carousel: rec, Academy, camps, leagues, tournaments, WPSL, financial aid (pinned everywhere)
IG carousel + FB · photo/video/CTA rhythm
Best Player of All Time — U10 edition
TikTok debut + Reels · 30 sec · rankings
Week 2 — Meet the Club
Explainer Ep. 1: "What even is club soccer?"
YouTube + Reel cutdown · series launch
Coaches' playing days throwback
IG carousel · guess the coach
Touch challenge, coach-rated
TikTok + Reels · challenge

"What it actually costs" + financial aid walkthrough
FB + IG carousel · transparency, with receipts
Bi-weekly highlight reel #1
Reels + FB + TikTok · premium edit debut
Coaches vs. kids: best position on the field
TikTok + Reels · rankings · comment bait
Week 4 — The Pathway
Explainer Ep. 2: "What is the WPSL?" (national runner-up story)
YouTube + Reel · pathway content
Game day in the community photo set
IG carousel · groups, serious + fun
Rate these lineups: FC 26 edition
TikTok + Reels · gaming culture
Theme: show, don't say. The engine scales to four posts a week: highlight reels on rhythm, coach authority weekly, and the MSHSL explainer lands right as high-school season is in full swing.

Standing weekly layer (not shown above): IG and FB stories carry Level-3 announcements, polls, reshared parent posts, and same-day practice moments. Kit drops, milestone wins, WPSL news, and World Cup moments interrupt the calendar as Level-1 events whenever they happen.

09
Step by Step
Rollout & Measurement.
Three phases, and a scoreboard where every KPI maps to what Salvo actually cares about: registrations, visibility in underserved communities, and a brand people root for. Tap tasks to check them off.
1
Foundation — Weeks 1–2
Claim @salvosoccer on TikTok; align handles, bios (mission-first, not acronym-first) and link-in-bio across platforms
Recruit and brief team social media managers (Zoom one-on-ones, capture playbook walkthrough, skill-level tiering)
Purchase wireless mics; identify the comfortable-on-camera roster per team
Media waivers / photo consent collected and logged for every featured player — before the first shoot
Build the design system: announcement tiers, carousel templates, highlight-reel look, warm navy/gold/orange palette
Shoot the mission film (two practice visits + one game day + WPSL b-roll)
Set up the shared footage library with upload naming rules (team · date · shooter)
2
Launch & Learn — Month 1
Premiere the mission film + club carousel everywhere on the same morning; pin both; brief staff and parents to share
Run the Month 1 calendar (three posts a week + the stories layer)
Night-before calls before every planned shoot; FaceTime / WhatsApp support live
Reply to every comment and DM in the first 30 days — especially cost and registration questions
Weekly 30-minute review: what earned shares and saves, what died, which manager needs more direction
3
Make It a Habit — Months 2–3
Scale to four posts a week; batch capture on game days; lock the bi-weekly reel rhythm
Produce mini-doc #1; open the at-home UGC pipeline
Cross-promote the WPSL account into the main brand system
Monthly report against the KPI matrix; retire what's not working, double what is
Scope phase two: expanded graphics package (ESPN-style announcements, motion design), winter indoor content plan

The scoreboard.

ObjectiveContent It Maps ToPrimary Metric90-Day Target
Reach new familiesEntertainment engine, challenges, rankingsShares, non-follower reach %, FYP impressions≥40% of reach from non-followers on TikTok / Reels
Registration pipelineExplainers, cost transparency, tryout contentLink clicks, DMs, saves on explainer postsMeasurable lift in registration-page traffic from social
Underserved-community visibilityAffordability posts, world-game content, multilingual captionsShares into community groups, DM inquiries about financial aidFinancial-aid inquiries via social tracked monthly, from zero
Brand beliefMission film, club carousel, mini-docsWatch time, follows per view, sentiment in commentsMission film ≥60% average watch; +25% follower growth
Insider prideHighlight reels, name/team/position posts, throwbacksStory reshares, tags, parent sharesEvery team featured at least twice in 90 days

Remember: follower count is the vanity metric. The wins that matter: a parent in a WhatsApp group forwarding our cost explainer, a kid begging to be in the next edit, a dad DMing "is financial aid real?" — and getting an answer in an hour. Entertain, inform, inspire. That's the job.

Anecdotal × Salvo SC
Entertain. Inform.
Inspire.
Prepared by Anecdotal Media for Salvo Soccer Club · July 2026 · Confidential
